title: Cutting a quill pen from a flight feather
subtitle: The pre-1800 writing instrument, still useful, still precise
excerpt: A quill pen cut from a large flight feather writes with a responsiveness that metal nibs rarely match. The shaft flexes under the hand; the cut tip holds ink differently from steel. The cutting process takes under ten minutes once you have the right feather and a sharp knife.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on tools and materials, Johnston gives detailed instructions for hardening and cutting quills, the method still used today. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Edward Cocker's seventeenth-century penmanship manuals also describe quill preparation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A writing pen made from the hollow shaft of a large bird's flight feather, the tip of which is cut to a broad-edge or pointed nib.
The standard method for cutting a quill nib: a long oblique slice along the back of the shaft, then a short square cut across the tip to form the writing edge.
Plunging a quill shaft briefly into hot sand or heating it to harden and semi-clarify the material, making it less likely to split when cut.

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use sharp blades only; change when they dull. Cut on a self-healing mat, non-cutting hand well clear of the blade.

content.text: Before metal nibs were manufactured, every pen was a
quill

. The manuscripts of the medieval period, the Book of Kells, the Lindisfarne Gospels, Carolingian court documents, were written with quills from geese, swans, and turkeys. Johnston cut and used them throughout his working life and described them as having a quality of touch that no metal nib quite replicated.
What you need

items.name: Large flight feather (primary wing feather from a goose or turkey)
items.substitutions: The five outermost primaries from a goose give the best quills. Right-hand writers traditionally prefer feathers from the left wing, so the quill curves away from the face.

items.name: Dry fine sand in a metal tray, or a craft heat tool
items.substitutions: Hardening is optional but strongly recommended for a beginner's first cuts

content.text: Hardening
the quill
A fresh or dried feather is often too soft and splits unpredictably when cut. Hardening makes the shaft more glassy and predictable.
Strip the barbs from the lower 10 to 12 cm of the shaft, the writing end.

Push the tip of the shaft into hot sand (at about 200°C) for 5 to 10 seconds. The shaft should become firm and semi-transparent. Alternatively, pass the tip briefly over a craft heat tool at 5 to 8 cm distance. Do not scorch or discolour the shaft.
Flatten the shaft slightly while still warm by pressing it against a flat surface with a smooth bone folder. This is optional but makes cutting easier.
The
Dutch cut

Place the quill shaft on the cutting mat, curved side (back) uppermost. Make the first cut: a long oblique slice starting about 25 mm from the tip, cutting in toward the centre of the shaft at a shallow angle. This removes a long wedge from the back of the shaft and exposes the hollow interior. Keep the knife almost parallel to the shaft.
Remove any membrane visible inside the hollow.
Make a shoulder cut on each side: two short cuts angling in from the edges toward the tip, narrowing the nib to the width required. For broad-edge calligraphy, leave 2 to 4 mm of nib width.

Place the nib on the cutting mat, hollow side up. Make the final cut: a single clean slice straight across the tip, perpendicular to the shaft. This is the writing edge. Aim for a clean, right-angled cut, not a tearing motion.
Slit the nib: using the knife tip, make a tiny slit 5 to 8 mm up from the tip along the centre line. This is the ink channel. If the slit is too long the nib will flood; too short it will scratch.
Testing the quill

Dip in walnut or iron-gall ink and write a stroke on cartridge paper. The quill should feed ink smoothly without flood or scratch. If it scratches, the writing edge is too sharp at the corner, make a tiny angle cut at each corner of the nib. If it floods, the slit is too long and the nib should be cut back and re-slit at a shorter length.
Common mistakes

items.symptom: Shaft splits along the side rather than cutting cleanly
items.cause: The shaft was not hardened, or the knife was not sharp enough.
items.fix: Harden the next feather before cutting. A fresh blade is essential, a dull blade tears rather than slices.

items.cause: The slit is too long or the nib is too wide.
items.fix: Cut back the tip a fraction and re-slit, starting shorter.

items.cause: No slit, or a slit too short, or the hollow is blocked with membrane.
items.fix: Check for membrane inside the hollow. Extend the slit slightly. Touch the nib to wet paper to prime it.
